scope_note: This panel uses Penn’s official academics, recommendation, writing, and testing pages. It is school-wide but includes Penn’s published school-specific academic-preparation guidance where relevant.

areas.name: Overall rigor
areas.summary: Penn wants applicants to take core academic subjects throughout all four years and challenge themselves based on what their school offers.
areas.detail: Penn encourages students to take English, social studies, math, science, and foreign language throughout high school and says it reviews rigor in the context of each school’s actual course offerings and opportunities.

areas.name: School-specific preparation
areas.summary: Penn publishes extra academic-preparation guidance for its undergraduate schools, especially around math and science readiness.
areas.detail: Penn says Engineering applicants should show strong preparation in physics and mathematics, particularly calculus; Nursing applicants should show strong preparation in chemistry and biology; and Wharton applicants should show strong preparation in mathematics, including exposure to calculus in high school.

areas.name: Recommendations
areas.summary: Penn requires one counselor/school-official recommendation and one core-subject teacher recommendation, with one extra letter optional.
areas.detail: Penn requires recommendations from a school counselor or other school administrator and a teacher in a core subject area, and it allows one additional recommendation from another teacher or community supporter only if it adds perspective not already in the application.

areas.name: Essays
areas.summary: Penn requires a personal statement, two Penn short-answer prompts for all applicants, and one school-specific prompt, with possible extra program essays.
areas.detail: Penn’s writing guidance says all applicants submit the main personal statement, two Penn prompts, and one undergraduate-school-specific prompt; coordinated dual-degree and specialized programs can require additional essays.
